methodology: 90.9% preseason projections + 9.1% cumulative roto performance through 2 weeks (2/22 of season). Each additional week proportionally increases the weight of real results.

rankings.blurb: Dead last in roto. Twelfth out of twelve. A roto score of 128 that looks like a golf score on a par-72 course. The offense is hitting .204 with a .350 SLG, the pitching has 60 strikeouts and a 4.71 ERA, and the only thing keeping this team alive is 11 saves+holds, which is apparently enough to power-rank first in a league where preseason projections carry 90.9% of the weight. The Week 2 tie with Balls to the Wall (6-6-4) was the most exciting display of mutual mediocrity this season. Judge, Soto, Harper, and Freeman are collectively cosplaying as replacement-level players through two weeks. The model still believes. The model is currently the only one.

The bats are fine -- 59 runs, 16 homers, 57 RBI, a .449 SLG. The pitching is a war crime. A 6.86 ERA. A 1.78 WHIP. Two wins. Zero quality starts. A 1.72 K/BB that suggests the staff is actively trying to load the bases before every pitch. ShawSwank Redemption demolished them 10-2-4 in Week 2, and honestly 2 category wins feels generous. Roto rank of 8th is entirely the pitching's fault -- dead last in five pitching categories. But preseason rank 2 at 90.9% weight means this team cruises at #2 in the power rankings while posting a losing record. The spreadsheet giveth and the spreadsheet absolutely does not care about your ERA.

The only undefeated team whose power rank and roto rank are actually in the same zip code. Eleven wins lead the league. A 3.22 ERA and 1.09 WHIP. A 3.38 K/BB. The pitching staff is doing things that should require a license. The 10-2-4 demolition of Young and Yandy in Week 2 was surgical -- and 10 stolen bases is second in the league, which nobody saw coming. The offense still has only 10 homers and 47 runs, which is the kind of production that makes you check if the lineup is using wiffle bats. But when your pitching is this dominant and you're 2-0 with an 18-7-7 category record, the blend formula and the eye test are finally shaking hands.

The Week 2 bounce-back was emphatic -- a 10-4-2 thrashing of Duran Duran after getting punked in Week 1. Fifteen homers through two weeks is solid, and 9 steals plus 55 RBI show the bats are finding their rhythm. But a .218 batting average and .294 OBP means this team is essentially hitting dingers and stealing bases while striking out the rest of the time, which is either a viable strategy or a cry for help depending on your perspective. Roto 5th and power rank 4th are close enough that nobody can complain about the blend here. Skenes and the pitching staff delivered a 3.42 ERA in Week 2 -- if that holds, the Spoons are legitimate.

After getting bodied 2-12-2 by Burns in Week 1, Caminero returned the favor to Camp of the New Orleans Saints with an 11-2-3 beatdown that was frankly rude. A cumulative 2.40 ERA and 1.03 WHIP are both best in the league. Nineteen homers lead the league too. Roto rank 3rd, power rank 5th -- the blend is holding them back by two spots, which at 9.1% actual weight is the system working as designed. The 2 stolen bases remain an embarrassment that no amount of ERA dominance can hide. If they could find someone willing to run, this roto rank would be even more disgusting. The Week 2 revenge tour suggests the Week 1 loss was the outlier, not the rule.

Wait...wait...wait...wait -- this team is 2-0, has the best category record in the entire league at 20-7-5, is ranked FIRST in roto by a comfortable margin with a score of 50.5, and is still sitting at sixth in the power rankings. The 8-5-3 win over CLAUDE 4 COMMISH in Week 2 was business as usual for a team batting .287 with a .381 OBP and a 3.10 ERA. Burns is first in R, AVG, OBP, and top-2 in ERA, SLG, and QS. The "weaknesses" listed are K/BB at 7th and RBI at 5.5 -- most teams would kill for those as their worst categories. At 9.1% weight, being roto #1 vs preseason #6 moves the blend to 5.55. Five more percentage points of real data and this team starts climbing. The spreadsheet is playing hard to get, and Burns is winning anyway.

The Week 2 tie with UBC (6-6-4) is peak Balls to the Wall energy -- not bad enough to lose outright, not good enough to actually win. Through two weeks: 0 wins, 1 loss, 1 tie. The 3.41 K/BB is second-best in the league, which is a nice stat to put on your refrigerator while your team posts a 4.22 ERA and 3 quality starts. The roto rank dropped from 5th to 9th as the early pitching magic wore off, and now the blend (7.18) is propped up almost entirely by the preseason projection of 7th. The offense is aggressively average across the board -- 49 runs, 13 homers, 45 RBI, nothing exciting, nothing catastrophic. This team is the human-trafficking equivalent of a beige sedan.

After a triumphant 10-3-3 Week 1 over PO' BOYD, reality arrived in the form of Senor Burns, who administered an 8-5-3 lesson in humility. The pitching remains elite -- 129 Ks lead the league, the 3.58 K/BB is best in the league, and 8 wins are third-best. But 43 runs and 40 RBI are both dead last in the entire league, which is the kind of offensive output that makes you wonder if the hitters are being paid by the strikeout. Roto 7th, one spot better than preseason 8th, blend barely moves to 7.91. The AI-managed team is performing exactly like an AI would -- hyper-optimized in three pitching categories, completely ignoring that runs and RBI exist.

rankings.team_name: Camp of the New Orleans Saints
rankings.blurb: Caminero Crush came in Week 2 and left nothing but scorched earth -- an 11-2-3 loss that dropped the cumulative category record to a grim 10-17-5. The roto rank of 6th is still three spots better than the preseason projection of 9th, but at 9.1% weight the blend doesn't budge from 8.73. Wait...wait...wait...wait -- this team has 62 runs (second in the league), a .260 AVG, and 7 quality starts, but also a 1.27 WHIP, only 2 saves+holds, and 4 wins. It's like having a sports car with three flat tires. The offensive foundation is there, but the pitching peripherals and bullpen production need to stop being a punchline before the blend ever takes notice.

PO' BOYD SANDWICH came into Week 2 and won 12-1-3 -- yes, twelve categories to one. That's not a baseball result, that's a restraining order. The Polar Express is now 0-2 with a 6-20-6 category record, which is the kind of cumulative damage that takes months to repair. A 5.12 ERA and 1.43 WHIP anchor the bottom of the pitching leaderboard, and 4 stolen bases suggest the baserunners have taken a vow of stillness. The lone bright spot is 7 saves+holds, which at this point is like bragging about your parallel parking skills while the car is on fire. Roto 11th, preseason 10th, blend 10th -- everything agrees this team is in trouble.

This is the single most disrespected team in the power rankings and it's not even close. Roto rank: 2nd. Power rank: 11th. That's a NINE-SPOT gap between actual performance and where the blend puts them. PO' BOYD obliterated The Polar Express 12-1-3 in Week 2 -- winning twelve categories in a single week is the kind of dominance that should come with a trophy. The cumulative numbers are gorgeous: 60 runs, 17 homers, 58 RBI (most in the league), a .452 SLG (tied for best), a 3.23 ERA, and a 1.06 WHIP. But preseason rank 11 at 90.9% weight is an anchor made of depleted uranium. Even performing at a roto #2 pace, the blend only moves to 10.18. This team needs about ten more weeks of this before the model stops treating them like a preseason punchline.

Nine Spoons came to collect in Week 2 with a 10-4-2 victory that exposed every offensive flaw on this roster. A .196 batting average. A .280 OBP. A .306 SLG. Seven home runs through two weeks. This team's offense is essentially a stolen base delivery service -- 11 steals lead the league -- attached to a lineup that forgot how to make contact with a baseball. The 8 quality starts and 9 saves+holds show the pitching has a pulse, and the 3.64 ERA is respectable. But when your three worst categories are AVG, OBP, and SLG -- the three categories that describe whether your hitters can hit -- you have a foundational problem. Roto 10th, preseason 12th, blend 11.82 -- two spots of roto improvement that the model politely ignores.
